title: How Words Become Numbers
subtitle: Why AI turns language into math, and why that's nothing to fear
theme: Turn the scariest-sounding idea in AI — "language becomes math" — into something cozy, visual, and obvious, so you feel curious instead of intimidated.
learningObjectives: Explain in one plain sentence why a computer can only work with numbers, never with letters directly
Describe what an embedding is, using the idea of giving every word its own coordinates on a map
Walk through a tiny example of why similar words end up sitting close together
Understand 'matrix multiplication' as simply mixing piles of numbers to discover relationships, with zero math anxiety
Say clearly what this number trick actually buys us: an AI that can compare, group, and understand meaning
storyHook: Imagine handing a brilliant friend a love letter written in invisible ink. They want to help you, they really do, but they simply cannot see the words. Now imagine you had a magic lamp that turned every word into a glowing dot of light, and suddenly your friend could see, sort, and understand everything. That magic lamp is real, and today you'll learn exactly how it works. By the end, the phrase "AI turns words into math" will feel as friendly as turning a recipe into a shopping list.

slides.title: Welcome Back: Today We Open the Hood
slides.body:
Welcome back. Over the last three days you met some big ideas. On Day 2 you discovered **attention** — the AI's clever habit of deciding which words in a sentence matter most, like a reader highlighting the important bits. On Day 3 you met the **Transformer**, the engine built around that habit.

But we skipped over one quiet little secret, and today we finally open the hood to look at it.

Here is the puzzle. A Transformer is, at heart, a giant calculator. It adds and multiplies numbers, billions of times a second. Yet you type it words — "How was your day?" — and it answers in words. So something must be quietly translating between your world of language and its world of numbers.

That translator is today's whole story. It's called turning words into numbers, and it sounds intimidating. It isn't. By the end of this hour you'll find it almost obvious, maybe even beautiful. Think of today as learning how a foreign friend reads your letters by first turning each word into a color they can see.
slides.speakerNotes: Keep the energy warm and reassuring. Emphasize that today demystifies the one part beginners most fear: math.
slides.marketConnection: Every chatbot you used in 2026 — from customer-support bots to writing assistants — silently does this word-to-number translation before it can reply to you.

slides.title: Why Computers Only Speak Numbers
slides.body:
Let's start with the simplest fact, and it's a fact about all computers, not just AI.

Deep down, a computer is an enormous wall of tiny switches. Each switch is either on or off — that's it. We write those two states as 1 and 0. Everything a computer has ever done — your photos, your songs, this very sentence — is, underneath, just huge patterns of on-and-off switches. Numbers, in other words.

So a computer never truly "sees" the letter A. What it stores is a number that *stands for* A. Think of it like a coat-check at a theater: you hand over your coat, you get a numbered ticket. The cloakroom doesn't understand coats; it understands ticket numbers. Letters work the same way — each gets a numbered ticket so the machine can handle it.

This matters because language is the messiest, most human thing we have, full of feeling and nuance. For a computer to help us with it, language has to be checked in at the door and handed a numbered ticket first. The rest of today is about making those tickets *smart* — so the numbers don't just label words, they actually capture meaning.
slides.speakerNotes: Use the coat-check analogy slowly; it's the foundation for the 'smart tickets' idea later.
slides.marketConnection: When you dictate a text message to your phone in 2026, your voice is turned into numbers before any AI ever 'reads' it.

slides.title: From Dumb Tickets to Smart Coordinates
slides.body:
Giving each word a plain ticket number works, but it's a bit dumb. If "cat" gets ticket 7 and "kitten" gets ticket 4,300, those numbers tell the computer nothing about the two words being related. The numbers are just name tags pulled from a hat.

We want something cleverer. We want numbers where *closeness means similarity*.

Picture a map of a country. Every town has coordinates — a pair of numbers for how far east and how far north it sits. Towns near each other on the map have similar coordinates. You can tell two places are neighbors just by glancing at their numbers, without ever seeing the towns.

This is the big idea, and it has a name: an **embedding**. An embedding gives every word a set of coordinates, placing it as a dot on a giant invisible map of meaning. Words with similar meanings get placed near each other, so their numbers naturally come out similar.

So "cat" and "kitten" end up as close neighbors on this map, while "cat" and "helicopter" sit far apart. The computer can now measure meaning the same way you'd measure distance between two towns: by comparing their numbers.
slides.speakerNotes: Land the word 'embedding' here and immediately tie it to the map-coordinates picture.
slides.marketConnection: Search bars and shopping sites in 2026 use embeddings so that searching 'comfy couch' still finds a listing titled 'cozy sofa'.

slides.title: A Tiny Map You Can Actually Picture
slides.body:
Let's make this real with a map so small you can hold it in your head.

Imagine a flat map with just two directions. Left-to-right means "how royal is this word?" Bottom-to-top means "how male or female?" Two simple feelings, two coordinates.

Now drop in four words. **King** goes top-right: very royal, leaning male. **Queen** goes top-left: very royal, leaning female. **Man** goes bottom-right: not royal, leaning male. **Woman** goes bottom-left: not royal, leaning female.

Look at what just happened. The four words formed a neat rectangle. "King" and "queen" sit side by side because they share royalty. "King" and "man" stack together because they share maleness. The *positions* now carry meaning.

Here's the delightful part. If you start at "king," then slide the same way you'd go from "man" to "woman," you land almost exactly on "queen." The map has quietly captured the idea that king-is-to-man as queen-is-to-woman — pure relationship, expressed as movement on a map.

Real AI maps don't have two directions; they have hundreds. We can't draw those. But the principle is identical: meaning becomes position, and position becomes numbers.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the emotional high point — let learners feel the 'aha' of the king/queen rectangle before moving on.
slides.marketConnection: This exact king-queen relationship trick is how 2026 AI tools auto-suggest 'she/her' rewrites or translate gendered words correctly across languages.

slides.title: Why Hundreds of Directions, Not Just Two
slides.body:
Our little map had only two directions — royalty and gender. That's charming, but the real world of words is far richer than two feelings.

Think of a single word like "apple." Is it a food? A color hint? A famous company? Sweet or sour? Common or rare? No single pair of coordinates could capture all of that at once.

So real embeddings give each word not two numbers, but hundreds — often several hundred. Each one is like an extra question being asked about the word: How alive is it? How formal? How happy? How edible? You can't picture a map with hundreds of directions, and that's fine — *nobody* can. Even the engineers who build these systems can't visualize it.

Here's the comforting part: you don't need to. The rule that made our tiny map work — similar things sit close, related things move together — holds true no matter how many directions there are. A list of three hundred numbers is just a much richer address for a word, the way a full postal address pins down a house far more precisely than "somewhere up north."

More directions simply means more shades of meaning the AI can keep track of.
slides.speakerNotes: Reassure learners that inability to visualize high dimensions is universal and totally okay.
slides.marketConnection: When a 2026 music app says 'because you liked this song,' it's comparing rich multi-number embeddings of songs, not just genre labels.

slides.title: Mixing Numbers to Find Relationships
slides.body:
Now, the phrase that scares everyone: **matrix multiplication**. Deep breath. It's gentler than it sounds.

Forget the word "matrix." Just picture two neat piles of numbers — like two columns in a spreadsheet. Matrix multiplication is simply a recipe for *combining* those piles to produce a new set of numbers. That's the whole idea: a structured way of mixing numbers together.

Here's a kitchen analogy. Imagine making a smoothie. You have your ingredients (the word's coordinates) and you have a recipe card that says how much of each ingredient to use (the AI's learned settings). You blend them according to the recipe and out comes something new — a flavor that wasn't in any single fruit alone. Matrix multiplication is that blender: ingredients in, recipe applied, fresh result out.

Why does AI do this constantly? Because mixing a word's numbers with the numbers of nearby words is how the AI discovers *relationships*. It's how "bank" near "river" comes out tasting different from "bank" near "money." The blending lets context flavor each word.

So "matrix multiplication" is really just: blend the number-piles by a recipe to reveal how things relate. No fear required.
slides.speakerNotes: Hammer the smoothie/blender image. Avoid any actual arithmetic on screen.
slides.marketConnection: Every time an AI in 2026 figures out whether 'Apple released' means the company or the fruit, this number-blending is doing the work.

slides.title: What This Whole Trick Actually Buys Us
slides.body:
Step back and admire what we've built, brick by brick.

We started with a cold fact: computers only handle numbers. We could have stopped at dumb ticket numbers — but instead we gave every word smart coordinates on a giant map of meaning, an embedding. Then we let the AI blend those numbers together to reveal relationships.

So what did all that effort actually buy us? Three lovely powers.

First, **comparison**. The AI can now measure how similar two words or sentences are, just by checking how close their numbers sit — like spotting neighbors on a map.

Second, **grouping**. It can gather related ideas together, which is how it knows "doctor, nurse, hospital" belong in one neighborhood.

Third, **understanding by context**. By mixing a word's numbers with its neighbors', the AI lets surrounding words flavor each word's meaning, so language stops being a list of isolated labels and starts behaving like connected thought.

Here's the reassuring takeaway. The AI isn't doing scary math at you. It's doing the most human thing imaginable — noticing what's similar, what's related, what belongs together — and it just happens to do that bookkeeping with numbers. Math is the tool, not the point. Meaning is the point.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame math as a humble servant of meaning; this reframe is the day's core comfort.
slides.marketConnection: Spam filters, resume screeners, and plagiarism checkers in 2026 all run on this 'compare the numbers' superpower under the hood.

slides.title: Recap: The Magic Lamp, Explained
slides.body:
Remember the magic lamp from the start — the one that turned invisible words into glowing dots your friend could finally see? You now know exactly how that lamp works.

Let's retrace the path in plain words.

Computers only understand numbers, because deep down they're just walls of on-off switches. So before AI can touch your language, every word gets checked in and handed a number — like a coat at a coat-check.

But plain numbers are dumb, so we upgrade them into **embeddings**: smart coordinates that place each word as a dot on a vast map of meaning, where similar words sit close, just like neighboring towns share similar map coordinates.

Our tiny king-queen-man-woman map showed how positions can capture not just meaning but *relationships* — and real maps simply use hundreds of directions instead of two.

Then **matrix multiplication**, that scary-sounding phrase, turned out to be a friendly blender: mix the number-piles by a recipe to reveal how words relate in context.

And the payoff? AI can now compare, group, and understand language. Math is just the quiet bookkeeping behind a very human skill.

You opened the hood today, and nothing in there was scary after all.
slides.speakerNotes: Close warmly and confidently; the learner should feel they conquered the 'math' fear.
slides.marketConnection: The embeddings you learned about today are the same building blocks powering the recommendation, search, and chat features you'll touch dozens of times this week.

keyTakeaways: Computers only work with numbers because they are built from tiny on-off switches; words must be turned into numbers before any AI can use them.
An embedding gives every word its own coordinates on a giant map of meaning, so similar words end up sitting close together — like neighboring towns sharing similar map positions.
The tiny king-queen-man-woman map shows that positions can capture relationships, not just single words; real AI maps use hundreds of directions instead of two.
'Matrix multiplication' is just a friendly recipe for blending piles of numbers together to reveal how words relate — think of a smoothie blender, not scary math.
This whole trick buys the AI three powers: comparing, grouping, and understanding words by their context.
Math is only the quiet bookkeeping; the real point is meaning — noticing what's similar, related, and belongs together, which is a deeply human skill.
marketSnapshot2026: By 2026, embeddings have quietly become the backbone of almost everything you touch online. When you search a shopping site for 'comfy couch' and it finds a 'cozy sofa,' when your music app explains a recommendation, when a customer-support bot understands your typo-filled question, or when your email auto-sorts a receipt into the right folder — embeddings are doing the matching. Companies now run massive 'vector databases' whose only job is storing billions of these word-and-document coordinates and finding nearest neighbors in milliseconds. The skill of turning text into good embeddings is so valuable that it's sold as a standalone service by every major AI provider.
homework: Pick any three everyday words — for example 'dog,' 'puppy,' and 'airplane.' On a piece of paper, draw a simple two-direction map like our king-queen one (you choose the two directions, such as 'how big' and 'how alive'). Place your three words as dots. Notice which two land close together and which one sits far away — that closeness is exactly what an embedding captures, just with hundreds of directions instead of two. Bring your little map to Day 5.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow, on Day 5, we finally meet the brain-like machine that does all this number-mixing: the neural network. You've seen what the numbers are — now you'll see the surprisingly simple gadget that learns from them. No biology degree required, promise.
